table-585-585.caption: Write all the comments left by users who edited the post titled 'Why square the difference instead of taking the absolute value in standard deviation?'
data: Squaring makes the function differentiable, which is crucial for optimization.
It also gives more weight to larger deviations, reflecting their greater impact.
So, it's not just about making everything positive, but also about calculus?
Exactly. The L2 norm (squaring) is preferred for its mathematical properties.
Is there any situation where using absolute value would be more appropriate?
For robustness against outliers, mean absolute deviation (MAD) can sometimes be used.
This explanation really clarifies a long-standing confusion for me. Thanks!
